name: Oya
description: Oya, also known as Ìyá Òyà, is a prominent Orisha, a spirit or deity, in the Yoruba religion and its diasporic traditions, particularly in Candomblé and Santería. She is a powerful and complex figure associated with winds, lightning, storms, and the Niger River (called Oya in Yoruba). Oya is often depicted as a fierce warrior woman, embodying strength, independence, and transformative change. She is considered a protector of women and a force for justice, fiercely defending those who are wronged. Her association with the marketplace signifies her control over commerce and the flow of goods. Oya is also strongly linked to the realm of the dead, serving as a psychopomp who guides souls to the afterlife and is a guardian of cemeteries. Her dual nature reflects both the destructive power of storms and the revitalizing force of renewal, highlighting her crucial role in the cycle of life and death. Oya's worship emphasizes respect for the forces of nature and the acceptance of change as a constant in the universe. She is often invoked for protection, guidance, and the ability to overcome obstacles.
subtitle: Goddess of Winds and Storms

keyMyths.title: Oya and Shango's Love
keyMyths.description: One of the most well-known myths surrounding Oya involves her relationship with Shango, the Orisha of thunder and lightning. Oya is often depicted as Shango's favorite wife and a powerful warrior in her own right. The myth details how Oya learned the secrets of Shango's power and how she wielded lightning herself. Their relationship is a complex blend of love, power, and rivalry, showcasing Oya's independence and her ability to stand as an equal to even the most powerful Orisha. Their bond is a cornerstone of Yoruba mythology and illustrates the dynamic interplay between different forces of nature.
keyMyths.source: Idowu, E. Bolaji. *Olódùmarè: God in Yoruba Belief.* Longman, 1962.

keyMyths.title: Oya and the Nine Children
keyMyths.description: Oya is often referred to as the 'Mother of Nine,' a title that stems from a myth about her giving birth to nine children, all of whom were stillborn except for one. This myth highlights Oya's connection to death and the afterlife, solidifying her role as a guide for souls transitioning between worlds. The story also demonstrates her resilience and her ability to persevere through immense loss, further emphasizing her strength and unwavering spirit. This myth is a poignant reminder of the cyclical nature of life and death, a central theme in Oya's worship.
keyMyths.source: Awolalu, J. Omosade. *Yoruba Beliefs and Sacrificial Rites.* Longman, 1979.

keyMyths.title: Oya's Role as a Warrior
keyMyths.description: Many tales portray Oya as a fierce warrior who defends her people and fights for justice. One such myth tells of Oya leading an army of women into battle, wielding a sword and controlling the winds to defeat her enemies. This myth showcases Oya's strength, courage, and unwavering commitment to protecting those under her care. It also highlights her association with female empowerment and her role as a symbol of defiance against oppression. The myth reinforces the idea that Oya is a force to be reckoned with, both in the physical and spiritual realms.
keyMyths.source: Bascom, William. *Sixteen Cowries: Yoruba Divination from Africa to the New World.* Indiana University Press, 1969.
